title: Floods
summary: What to do before, during, and after a flood. Adapted from official Ready.gov and U.S. National Weather Service (NWS) guidance. The life-saving rule: when you see moving water, Turn Around Don't Drown.

antes.heading: Know your area's risk
antes.body: If you live near a river, creek, dry channel, or in low ground, your risk is real even if 'nothing has ever happened'. Flash floods arrive in minutes from heavy rain upstream — even under clear sky where you are.

antes.heading: Define your route to high ground
antes.body: Identify now the nearest high point you can reach on foot within minutes. Agree on it with your family as the meeting point. In a flash flood there is no time to decide.

antes.heading: Protect documents and prep the kit
antes.body: Documents and medications in a waterproof bag, stored high in the house. Emergency kit ready to grab and go (list below).

antes.heading: Heed official notices
antes.body: A flood watch means flooding is possible; a warning means it is happening or imminent — act immediately. Follow civil protection and local news whenever rain is heavy.

durante.heading: Turn Around Don't Drown — never cross moving water
durante.body: Six inches of moving water can knock a person down; one to two feet will carry away a vehicle, even a truck. You cannot know the depth or whether the road still exists underneath. Most flood deaths are vehicles that tried to cross.

durante.heading: Get to high ground immediately
durante.body: If there is a flash-flood warning or you see water rising, move NOW to your high point. Don't wait for confirmation or gather belongings. The first minutes are the difference.

durante.heading: If water enters the house
durante.body: Shut off electricity at the main breaker if safe. Move to the highest livable level — avoid closed attics with no exit where you could be trapped. Roof only if necessary, and signal your position (bright cloth, flashlight, whistle).

durante.heading: Avoid contact with the water
durante.body: Floodwater hides holes, debris, and animals, and can be energized by downed lines or contaminated with sewage. If you must walk through still water, probe the ground ahead with a stick.

despues.heading: Don't return until the official all-clear
despues.body: Structures can be undermined and roads washed out underneath even when they look fine. Wait for the authorities' announcement before returning to a flooded area.

despues.heading: Treat all water as contaminated
despues.body: Don't use floodwater for anything. Discard any food it touched, boil or purify drinking water until authorities confirm it's safe, and wash everything the water reached.

despues.heading: Electricity and gas before entering
despues.body: Never enter a flooded home with the power connected. If you smell gas, don't go in: report it. Use a flashlight — never candles or matches — to inspect.

despues.heading: Document and report
despues.body: Photograph damage before cleaning. Mold appears within 24-48 hours: dry and ventilate as soon as possible. If the structure is compromised, request an assessment through Vigil.

supplyChecklist.qty: 1 gal per person per day × 3 days

supplyChecklist.note: To signal your position if stranded
Visible signal for rescuers from roof or window
